title: Apply: alpha, beta, and gamma decay
description: Apply your understanding of alpha, beta, and gamma decay in this set of free practice questions.
topic_path: Apply: alpha, beta, and gamma decay

assessment_items.question_content:
The radioisotope polonium-$210$ is used in devices that remove static electricity. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{210}_{84}Po} \rightarrow \ce{^{206}_{82}Pb} + \ce{^{4}_{2}He}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: This vent-type static eliminator uses polonium-210.
options.caption: *Vent-type static eliminator*

assessment_items.question_content:
The radioisotope bismuth-$209$ is created inside red giant stars nearing the end of their lives. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{209}_{83}Bi} \rightarrow \ce{^{205}_{81}Tl} + \ce{^{4}_{2}He}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Mira A](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seeing_into_the_Heart_of_Mira_A_and_its_Partner.jpg)” by Ramstedt and W. Vlemmings, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)

options.alt: The star Mira-A is near the end of its life.
options.caption: *The star Mira A*

assessment_items.question_content:
The radioisotope radium-$223$ is used in certain medical treatments. It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{223}_{88}Ra} \rightarrow \ce{^{219}_{86}Rn} + \ce{^{4}_{2}He}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The radioisotope cesium-$137$ is produced in nuclear reactors, and is one of the most common components of nuclear waste. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{137}_{55}Cs} \rightarrow \ce{^{137}_{56}Ba} + \ce{^{0}_{-1}e} + \overline{\nu}_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: “[Advanced Test Reactor](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Advanced_Test_Reactor.jpg)” by Argonne National Laboratory, [CC BY-SA 2.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0/deed.en)

options.alt: The Advanced Test Reactor (ATR) core at Idaho National Laboratory
options.caption: *A small reactor core*

assessment_items.question_content:
The radioisotope carbon-$14$ can be used to find the age of textiles, bones, and mummies.

[[☃ image 1]]

 It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{14}_{6}C} \rightarrow \ce{^{14}_{7}N} + \ce{^{0}_{-1}e} + \overline{\nu}_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Tutankhamun's mummy on display in his tomb in Egypt
options.caption: *Tutankhamun's mummy*

assessment_items.question_content:
One of the radioisotopes released at Chernobyl was iodine-$131$, which causes harm when absorbed by the thyroid gland. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{131}_{53}I} \rightarrow \ce{^{131}_{54}Xe} + \ce{^{0}_{-1}e} + \overline{\nu}_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A helicopter photo of Chernobyl after the 1986 accident.
options.caption: *Chernobyl after the accident*

assessment_items.question_content:
The radioisotope magnesium-$23$ is used to study the energy produced when electrons and anti-electrons annihilate each other. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{23}_{12}Mg} \rightarrow \ce{^{23}_{11}Na} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: These bubble chamber tracks show antimatter, matter, and gamma radiation interacting.
options.caption: *Electron and positron tracks in a bubble chamber*

assessment_items.question_content:
Japanese scientists have found that lightning can change atmospheric nitrogen into the radioisotope nitrogen-$13$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{13}_{7}N} \rightarrow \ce{^{13}_{6}C} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The radioisotope rubidium-$82$ is used to create images of the myocardium, or human heart muscle.

[[☃ image 1]]

It decays by the following equation:

$\ce{^{82}_{37}Rb} \rightarrow \ce{^{82}_{36}Kr} + \ce{^{0}_{+1}e} + \nu_e$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Scanning machine for positron emission tomography
options.caption: *PET scanner*

assessment_items.question_content:
The following equation shows the radioactive decay of an excited nickel-$60$ nucleus.

$\ce{^{60}_{28}Ni^*} \rightarrow \ce{^{60}_{28}Ni}  + \ce{^{0}_{0}\gamma}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The following equation shows the radioactive decay of an excited protactinium-$234$ nucleus.

$\ce{^{234}_{91}Pa^*} \rightarrow \ce{^{234}_{91}Pa}  + \ce{^{0}_{0}\gamma}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The following equation shows the radioactive decay of an excited argon-$40$ nucleus.

$\ce{^{40}_{18}Ar^*} \rightarrow \ce{^{40}_{18}Ar}  + \ce{^{0}_{0}\gamma}$

**What type of decay is represented by the equation above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
